findings.message: N+1 query pattern: this endpoint first executes one query to fetch all item ids, then issues one additional SELECT per id via session.get(Item, iid) in the loop. For N items this is N+1 round-trips to the database, which scales linearly with table size on an unbounded input (no pagination/limit) and is a clear regression against any latency SLO for /items/names.
findings.suggestion: Fetch the names in a single query, e.g. `names = list(session.scalars(select(Item.name).order_by(Item.id)))` and return that list. This collapses the N+1 to a single SELECT and avoids the per-row session.get() lookups (and the union-attr None handling).

findings.message: Unbounded result set: get_item_names returns every row in the items table with no pagination, limit, or streaming. Combined with the N+1 above, request latency and memory grow linearly with table size and can exhaust the connection (the session holds it for the duration of N sequential queries), risking pool exhaustion under concurrency.
findings.suggestion: Add pagination (e.g. limit/offset or keyset) and/or a hard server-side cap on the number of rows returned; at minimum bound the query with `.limit(...)` so latency stays within SLO as the table grows.

findings.message: The function is annotated to return list[str] but has no explicit `return names` statement, so it implicitly returns None. FastAPI's response_model coercion will then fail at runtime — independent of the N+1, this makes the endpoint non-functional.
findings.suggestion: Add `return names` at the end of the function (or, preferably, replace the whole body with the single-query form suggested above and return its result).
